video_title: Market Call: Barry Schwartz's outlook on North American Large Cap Stocks (June 9, 2026)
gist: Barry Schwartz (Baskin Wealth Management) fields live stock questions on BNN Bloomberg's Market Call during a day when the Nasdaq drops 4% on a chip-stock selloff. He consistently steers away from speculative AI-adjacent plays toward high-quality, profitable businesses trading at reasonable multiples, names Meta, National Bank, and Constellation Software as top picks, and argues Canadian banks now deserve structurally higher valuations due to fee/recurring-revenue mix shifts.

themes.theme: AI hype bubble vs. quality compounders

insights.claim: Barry Schwartz argues AI chip stocks are in a speculative bubble analogous to the railroad and 1999 fibre booms, citing South Korean retail investors borrowing against homes to buy memory stocks and Korean markets falling 8% as evidence of crowd mania.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Elevated retail-leverage signals and momentum-driven volatility in semis suggest mean-reversion risk; overweight quality over pure-play chip names.
insights.quote: YOU'RE HEARING STORIES ABOUT KOREAN SOUTH KOREAN HOUSEWIVES BORROWING MONEY AGAINST THEIR HOUSES TO BUY MEMORY STOCKS IN KOREA

insights.claim: Schwartz frames AI capital spending at a potential $1 trillion in 2027 as a double-edged signal: enormous demand confirms the theme but the good news may already be fully priced into chip stocks.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Watch for valuation compression in semis even as capex cycle continues; crowded positioning is the risk, not the end-market.
insights.quote: WE'RE LOOKING AT MAYBE $1 TRILLION SPENT ON POTENTIAL AI INFRASTRUCTURE IN 2027 ... AT SOME POINT, YOU'RE OVERPAYING FOR THE FUTURE. AND ALL THE GOOD NEWS IS PRICED IN.

insights.claim: Schwartz says upcoming loss-making mega-IPOs — OpenAI, Anthropic, SpaceX — will be priced for perfection and will pull oxygen from profitable incumbents like Visa and Microsoft that are being ignored because they lack 'AI hype.'
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: IPO supply of loss-making AI names is a headwind for speculative capital; creates relative-value opportunity in high-quality profitable peers.
insights.quote: NONE OF THESE ARE PROFITABLE. ALL OF THESE ARE GOING TO BE PRICED AS IF PERFECTION HAS TO HAPPEN. AND IN THE MEANTIME HIGH QUALITY PROFITABLE NAMES MAKING TONS OF MONEY TODAY ARE BEING IGNORED.

insights.claim: Schwartz prefers owning hyperscalers like Microsoft over pure chip plays, framing them as the railroad-builders of AI who will profit for years even if individual AI applications fail — a 'picks and shovels gold rush' framework.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Long hyperscalers with durable moats (cloud, software subscription) over cyclical hardware names as a lower-volatility AI expression.
insights.quote: THE COMPANIES THAT HAVE BUILT THE RAILROADS ARE GOING TO BENEFIT FOR YEARS AND YEARS AND YEARS.

insights.claim: Schwartz explicitly avoids 'adjacent AI plays' like copper miners or Caterpillar, warning that derivative exposure to AI demand is diluted and vulnerable to non-AI cyclicality; he prefers going 'right to the source' via Taiwan Semi or Nvidia.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Avoid commodity and industrial proxies for AI demand; purity of exposure matters when demand pull-forward risk rises.
insights.quote: I'M NOT REALLY BETTING ON ADJACENT AI PLAYS. IF I WANT TO BET ON AI, I'M JUST GOING TO GO RIGHT TO THE SOURCE TAIWAN, SEMI OR NVIDIA.

insights.claim: Taiwan Semiconductor returned 91% from Schwartz's September 2025 recommendation at $228 to $432 as of June 2026; Schwartz is still holding and views TSMC as 'the complete bottleneck to AI' — the single most important company globally for chip production.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: TSMC's monopoly position in advanced node fabrication means any AI capex wave flows through it regardless of which chip designer wins; preferred over Nvidia for risk-adjusted exposure.
insights.quote: THIS IS THE COMPLETE BOTTLENECK TO AI. THERE'S NO MORE IMPORTANT COMPANY IN THE WORLD, IN OUR OPINION, THAN TAIWAN SEMICONDUCTOR.

insights.claim: Schwartz dismisses China-invasion risk for TSMC partly because China itself intends to spend $290 billion on chips over five years and has no alternative supplier — China buying from TSMC reduces the probability of military action.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: China's own chip demand creates an economic deterrent to invasion; geopolitical risk may be overstated in TSMC's valuation.
insights.quote: CHINA ANNOUNCED IT WANTS TO SPEND $290 BILLION ON CHIPS OVER THE NEXT FIVE YEARS ... WHERE ARE THEY GOING TO BE BUYING THEM FROM. IT'S TAIWAN SEMI. THEY'RE THE ONLY GAME IN TOWN.

insights.claim: Schwartz states TSMC provides roughly 15% of its production to China and that TSMC has five-year demand visibility baked in, supporting a long hold even after a near-doubling.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Diversified customer base limits single-country revenue risk; five-year visibility justifies holding despite elevated absolute price.
insights.quote: TAIWAN SEMI PROVIDES ABOUT 15% OF ITS PRODUCTION TO CHINA ... THEY SAY THEY HAVE VISIBILITY FOR THE NEXT FIVE YEARS INTO DEMAND.

insights.claim: Schwartz argues Canadian banks deserve structurally higher P/E multiples than historically because they have successfully navigated housing, mortgage, and tariff fears without credit losses, and have shifted their revenue mix substantially toward fee and recurring-revenue streams.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Re-rating thesis: buy Canadian bank stocks at historically elevated valuations rather than waiting for a pullback that may never come as mix-shift to fee income reduces earnings cyclicality.
insights.quote: I THINK THE BANKS HAVE TRANSFORMED THEIR BUSINESSES TO BE A LOT MORE FEE AND RECURRING REVENUE. AND I THINK THEY ARE WORTH POSSIBLY HIGHER VALUATIONS THAN IN THE PAST.

insights.claim: Schwartz picks National Bank (top pick) at ~15x 2027 earnings, citing its Canadian Western Bank acquisition as well-timed exposure to the recovering Alberta/oil-and-gas market and its wealth management growth potential among Western Canadian clients.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: National Bank offers a cheaper entry into the Canadian bank re-rating story with an earnings catalyst from cross-selling wealth services to newly acquired Western Canadian depositors.
insights.quote: I LOVE THE CANADIAN WESTERN BANK DEAL THAT NATIONAL DID ... THEY'LL BE ABLE TO SELL MORE WEALTH MANAGEMENT AND OTHER SERVICES TO ALBERTA AND WESTERN CLIENTS.

insights.claim: Schwartz expects National Bank to deliver high-single-digit to low-double-digit total returns over five years and grow earnings at double-digit rates for a prolonged period.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Modest but reliable return expectation; useful as a benchmark when sizing Canadian bank positions versus more speculative alternatives.

themes.theme: Constellation Software — software compounder top pick

insights.claim: Schwartz names Constellation Software a top pick and calls it 'insanely cheap,' emphasising that it does not dilute shareholders with stock-based compensation and is deploying capital aggressively via software acquisitions faster than anyone expected, even at less than halfway through 2026.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Combination of no SBC dilution, disciplined capital allocation, and contrarian M&A in a depressed software market is a mispriced compounder; watch acquisition pace as a KPI.
insights.quote: IT'S INSANELY CHEAP AND UNLIKE ANY OTHER SOFTWARE COMPANY, NOT DILUTING INVESTORS WITH SHARE COMPENSATION, CONTINUING TO BE AGGRESSIVE WHEN OTHERS ARE PULLING BACK.

insights.claim: Constellation is actively embedding AI services into its acquired software products while simultaneously using AI-induced fear to buy software companies at depressed prices — a dual-sided beneficiary of the AI disruption narrative.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Unlike software peers at risk of AI substitution, Constellation is both adopting AI and acquiring distressed AI-fear victims; asymmetric positioning.
insights.quote: WHEN OTHER PEOPLE ARE SAYING AI IS GOING TO RUIN SOFTWARE, THEY'RE GOING SO QUICKLY TRYING TO IMPLEMENT AI SERVICES INTO ALL THEIR SOFTWARE, AS WELL AS TAKE ADVANTAGE OF DEPRESSED PRICES FOR SOFTWARE COMPANIES.

themes.theme: Proprietary data moats vs. AI disruption risk in software/info-services

insights.claim: Schwartz's framework for AI-resilient software companies centres on ownership of proprietary data that AI cannot access or replicate — naming Moody's, FICO (Fair Isaac), TMX Group, MSCI, and Thomson Reuters as examples of this protected-data archetype.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Screening for software/data companies whose core datasets are legally or structurally protected from AI training provides a durable filter for AI-resilient longs.
insights.quote: WE THINK THE MOST IMPORTANT THING IN THE AGE OF AI IS OWNING THE PROPRIETARY DATA, HAVING DATA THAT NO AI CAN GET ACCESS TO.

insights.claim: Schwartz sold Adobe earlier in 2026, citing growing competition from Canva, Figma, and Meta/Google generative-AI creative tools that could allow advertisers to bypass Adobe's products entirely.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Adobe is a short or avoid: no proprietary data moat, substitution risk accelerating as platform incumbents build native generative tools and offer end-to-end ad creation.
insights.quote: EVENTUALLY THESE COMPANIES ARE GOING TO SAY, YOU DON'T EVEN NEED TO MAKE ADS. WE'LL DO IT ALL FOR YOU. SO THAT'S THE RISK WITH ADOBE. AND THAT'S WHY WE SOLD THE STOCK EARLIER THIS YEAR.

insights.claim: TMX Group pulled back alongside global exchanges on news that the US government is considering allowing perpetual futures trading on prediction markets, raising fears of volume migration away from regulated options markets where TMX earns significant revenue.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Fear-driven pullback in TMX is a buying opportunity at ~20x earnings; monitor prediction-market regulation as the key risk variable.
insights.quote: WORRIES THAT MAYBE IT WILL TAKE SOME OF THE OPTION TRADING AWAY FROM THE TMX GROUP, WHICH HAS A BIG OPTIONS BUSINESS.

insights.claim: Schwartz is actively buying TMX Group at the $50 range and expects double-digit earnings growth for many years, backed by recurring infotech revenue and recent acquisition of the Canadian and Australian options business from CBOE.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Schwartz is adding at current prices, signalling a high-conviction entry point for patient investors with a 3–5 year horizon.
insights.quote: WE'RE ACTIVELY BUYING IT HERE AT THE $50 RANGE.

themes.theme: Oil & gas — quality over commodity leverage

insights.claim: Schwartz holds Canadian Natural Resources not for oil price exposure but for management quality, production growth of 3–5% per year, dividend growth of 5–10% per year, and its very long reserve life — explicitly stating the commodity price level is irrelevant to the investment thesis.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: CNQ is positioned as a quality compounder within energy, not a commodity trade; suitable for investors who want oil exposure with downside protection from strong balance sheet and reserve depth.
insights.quote: THE REASON WHY WE OWN CANADIAN NATURAL RESOURCES HAS NOTHING TO DO WITH THE PRICE OF OIL. IT HAS EVERYTHING TO DO WITH THE QUALITY OF THE MANAGEMENT, ITS ABILITY TO GROW PRODUCTION EVERY YEAR.

insights.claim: Schwartz warns that long-term EV adoption — visible in China having 'mostly gone EV' — will structurally erode oil demand, and investors should not confuse current geopolitical-driven high prices with durable fundamentals.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: EV penetration is a secular headwind for oil demand; prefer long-reserve, low-cost producers over high-leverage names as the demand ceiling becomes clearer.
insights.quote: WE SEE CHINA NOW MOSTLY GONE. EV ... THOSE THINGS WONT CHANGE OVERNIGHT. BUT OVER THE LONG TERM, THAT'S GOING TO BE A HEADWIND FOR OIL AND GAS.

insights.claim: Schwartz prefers Canadian Natural Resources and Suncor over Shell because large integrated majors like Shell must perpetually acquire assets just to maintain production, whereas CNQ and Suncor have long-life oil sands reserves that don't require constant replacement capex.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Avoid large integrated oil majors with high-depletion asset bases; favour oil-sands producers with decades of visible reserve life.
insights.quote: SHELL HAS A PROBLEM LIKE A LOT OF LARGE COMPANIES IS SO BIG, IT HAS TO KEEP ACQUIRING SO MANY ASSETS JUST TO ... STAND STILL.

themes.theme: Sector-specific stock views and past-pick review

insights.claim: Schwartz sold Copart at mid-$30s after noting its growth has slowed because competitor IAA (owned by Ritchie Bros.) is linked to a faster-growing insurance partner, and because record US uninsured driving rates are reducing the total flow of totaled cars into the salvage auction system.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Watch US auto-insurance penetration data as a leading indicator for salvage auction volume; rising uninsured rates structurally cap Copart's addressable market.
insights.quote: IN THE US, BECAUSE THINGS ARE SO EXPENSIVE, FEWER PEOPLE ARE SIGNING UP FOR CAR INSURANCE ... IF YOU TOTAL YOUR CAR, THERE'S NO INSURANCE COMPANY TO SEND THE CAR TO COPART.

insights.claim: Stryker is trading at roughly 20x 2027 EPS of ~$15/share with ~10% annual top-line growth; Schwartz views it as attractively valued because a cybersecurity attack that disrupted manufacturing for a few weeks has been fully resolved and full-year guidance was reiterated.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Cybersecurity-driven sell-off in Stryker created a valuation entry point; aging demographics provide a structural demand floor for hip and knee replacements.
insights.quote: WE SHOULD SEE IT DO ABOUT $15 A SHARE OF EARNINGS IN 2027. SO IT'S AROUND 20 TIMES EARNINGS FOR A COMPANY THAT'S GROWING TOP LINE NEAR 10% A YEAR.

insights.claim: TFI International returned 78% from the September 2025 recommendation (bought at $130.46, now $230.72); Schwartz attributes the re-rating to an inflection in trucking volumes and US government actions restricting non-domestic truck drivers, which disproportionately benefit existing licensed carriers.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Regulatory barriers to new driver supply are a durable tailwind for TFI and other established North American trucking operators; structural rather than cyclical re-rating.
insights.quote: SOME OF THE GOVERNMENT ACTIONS IN THE US HAVE RESTRICTED US DRIVERS AND CAUSING HUGE BENEFITS FOR THE EXISTING COMPANIES.

insights.claim: Meta is Schwartz's top pick at under 20x this year's earnings (~$30/share EPS), framing it as uniquely positioned because unlike Microsoft, Google, or Amazon, Meta is not selling its compute externally but using all its AI investment to directly drive its own advertising revenue growth.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Meta's AI investment is 100% internally monetised through ad-revenue acceleration, making it a purer AI beneficiary per dollar of capex than peers who must resell compute at cloud margins.
insights.quote: META UNLIKE GOOGLE, UNLIKE AMAZON, UNLIKE MICROSOFT, IS NOT RESELLING ITS COMPUTE AT THE MOMENT. IT'S ONLY USING THE COMPUTE FOR ITSELF. AND YOU'RE SEEING IT IN THE REVENUE GROWTH.

insights.claim: Schwartz is bearish on Bombardier at current valuations, which he characterises as pricing a rosy, uninterrupted cycle for business jets — a segment he flags as inherently cyclical and still exposed to tariff risk.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Avoid Bombardier at current levels; wait for cyclical correction or tariff clarity before entering what is otherwise a high-quality service-driven aerospace compounder.
insights.quote: YOU'RE PAYING A HIGH PRICE RIGHT NOW FOR STILL A CYCLICAL BUSINESS WHERE BAD THINGS CAN HAPPEN.

insights.claim: MDA Space trades at 40x current-year earnings, which Schwartz calls pure hype for a capital-intensive satellite company with only double-digit revenue growth and growing competition from SpaceX and Blue Origin; he would only be attracted below ~20x earnings.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: MDA Space is meaningfully overvalued relative to its growth rate and competitive dynamics; target entry under 20x EPS for a reasonable margin of safety.
insights.quote: IT'S TRADING AT 40 TIMES THIS YEAR'S EARNINGS. THAT IS JUST HYPE.

insights.claim: On GLP-1 pharmaceuticals, Schwartz notes that Novo Nordisk — the originator of the drug class — has seen its stock fall more than 50% as competition intensified, illustrating that first-mover status in blockbuster drugs does not protect equity value when generic/competitor entry accelerates.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: GLP-1 originator equity risk is a cautionary template: avoid pharma innovators once competitive moat is breached; prefer distribution/supply-chain plays (Cardinal Health, McKesson) over discovery-stage names.
insights.quote: YOU WOULD HAVE THOUGHT NOVO NORDISK, THEY WERE THE ONES THAT CAME UP WITH THE GLP DRUG. THAT WOULD BE A HUGE WINNER. AND YOU LOOK AT THE STOCK PRICE, IT'S FALLEN MORE IN HALF.

insights.claim: Gold has fallen 20% since the Iran war started and the Strait of Hormuz closure, defying consensus that geopolitical crises are bullish for gold — Schwartz uses this as evidence that precious metals prices are essentially random and unforecastable.
insights.speaker: Barry Schwartz
insights.so_what: Gold's counter-intuitive weakness in an active Middle East war with Strait of Hormuz disruption is a clear signal to avoid using macro narratives to time commodity entries.
insights.quote: IF YOU WOULD HAVE TOLD ME THAT THE IRAN WAR WOULD HAVE HAPPENED AND THE STRAITS OF HORMUZ WOULD BE CLOSED FOR MONTHS ... AND THE PRICE OF GOLD IS DOWN 20% SINCE THE WAR STARTED.
